  "opinion": "I've seen plenty of misinformation online over the years, even though I don't use it much myself. It's frustrating because people can say anything without accountability. Back in my day, things were more straightforward—people had names attached to their words. Now, with social media, you just have an avatar and no one really knows who you are. I get a bit depressed thinking about how truth is lost in the noise. But I know there's only so much I can do as someone dealing with electricians' work, not online stuff.\n\nI suggest starting by checking sources carefully. Like when I read old Hindu texts for inspiration while painting—I make sure they're from reliable authors or institutions. My high agreeableness makes me want to help others see the truth, but my depression sometimes holds me back. Still, I believe in being conscientious about fact-checking.\n\nAnother thing is to use common sense. If something sounds too good or too bad to be true, it probably isn't. That's what worked for me when I was an electrician—never trust claims that don't hold up under scrutiny. My high extraversion might seem out of place online, but there are communities where people talk sensibly.\n\nIt helps to report fake content if you see it. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've seen plenty of misinformation online over the years, even though I don't use it much myself. It's frustrating because people can say anything without accountability. Back in my day, things were more straightforward—people had names attached to their words. Now, with social media, you just have an avatar and no one really knows who you are. I get a bit depressed thinking about how truth is lost in the noise. But I know there's only so much I can do as someone dealing with electricians' work, not online stuff.

I suggest starting by checking sources carefully. Like when I read old Hindu texts for inspiration while painting—I make sure they're from reliable authors or institutions. My high agreeableness makes me want to help others see the truth, but my depression sometimes holds me back. Still, I believe in being conscientious about fact-checking.

Another thing is to use common sense. If something sounds too good or too bad to be true, it probably isn't. That's what worked for me when I was an electrician—never trust claims that don't hold up under scrutiny. My high extraversion might seem out of place online, but there are communities where people talk sensibly.

It helps to report fake content if you see it. Although my time on social media is limited only a few times monthly, when I'm active, I can make reports easily enough.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: First, draw from personal experience with truth discernment in trades (electrician) and cultural background (Hinduism). Then, relate that to online interactions by comparing traditional communication methods with social media anonymity. Suggest practical steps like source verification and common sense application, tying them back to existing traits such as high conscientiousness and agreeableness for reliability.
